course_title: Literacy and Numeracy Across the Curriculum
course_description:
Literacy and numeracy are essential to students’ success in and beyond the classroom. Confident and critical use of language and mathematical knowledge, skills and dispositions, is necessary to their personal enablement, civic participation and to social equity. By providing meaningful opportunities for literacy and numeracy learning across the curriculum, teachers and school play a significant role in students’ development as literate and numerate citizens.

In this course, pre-service teachers will discuss contemporary theories, challenges and possibilities in literacy and numeracy teaching. They will explore and reflect on the nature of literacy and numeracy, including what it means to be literate and numerate in the 21st century, and learn how these general capabilities can be taught explicitly by all teachers in diverse classrooms, using appropriate subject matter expertise and effective pedagogical practices, including professional relationships with parents/carers. They will investigate and respond to literacy and numeracy demands in the curriculum and their teaching areas, with opportunities to critically evaluate relevant teaching strategies and their own task design.

Note : This course runs to the standard Semester 1 calendar for coursework, with the 10 days block professional experience commencing during Semester 1 central examinations period.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:
restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in BEd(Sec) dual programs
Weekly lectures are taught and the course Blackboard shared with the MTeach(Sec) cohort. Weekly tutorials are timetabled separately for each course.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Lectures and tutorials are scheduled weekly and designed for on campus attendance and engagement.
EDUC2630 is a core, 2-unit course. UQ recommends that students invest 10 hours per week in private study including contact time; in this case, seven hours per week of study, focusing on consolidating their understanding of teaching activities, engaging with course readings, and developing and completing assessment responses, in addition to three hours of prescribed weekly contact time. It is critical that pre-service teachers invest this time wisely to experience success in EDUC2630 and be prepared for future professional experience placements and graduate employment. To best support pre-service teachers in meeting course timing and requirements, targeted resources and guidance will be available early in the semester via the course Blackboard.
course_aims: This course aims to enhance students' knowledge and understanding of literacy and numeracy and to equip them with the knowledge and skills to understand the literacy and numeracy demands of their subject areas and to identify and capitalise on opportunities for literacy and numeracy teaching and learning. The 10 day block of professional experience aims to provide students with an opportunity for structured observation and hands-on classroom assistance.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the nature of literacy and numeracy in the 21st century, related issues and opportunities for effective pedagogical practice.
Demonstrate knowledge, understanding and analysis of explicit literacy and numeracy teaching and learning strategies and their application in their teaching areas.
Research, select and evaluate literacy and numeracy resources, including ICTs, to effectively engage students in literacy and numeracy learning in their teaching areas.

Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of strategies for involving parents/carers in their child’s literacy and numeracy learning and identify professional strategies to effectively engage with parents/carers sensitively and confidentially.
Demonstrate clear, fluent and coherent communication, which is appropriately referenced and consistent with personal, professional academic conventions.

Demonstrate and apply ethical practices appropriate for the teaching profession and an understanding of the place of legislative, school and employing authority requirements in the teaching and learning process.
Demonstrate an understanding of schools as workplaces and be familiar with the statutory, ethical and other requirements that govern the work of teachers in schools.
Demonstrate all teaching and professional requirements of the professional experience placement.

assessment_details.title: AT1 Core content modules
assessment_details.mode: Activity/ Performance, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia, Written
assessment_details.due_date: Module 1. 27/03/2026 2:00 pm Module 2. 24/04/2026 2:00 pm Module 3. 22/05/2026 2:00 pm Each module and timed assessment will be open to pre-service teachers three weeks before the due date and time.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: To develop and assess your knowledge and understanding of core content for this course, you are required to complete three online modules: Module 1 — With the brain in mind: explicit reading instruction using phonemic awareness and phonics Module 2 — Effective numeracy teaching and learning: Mathematics strands and proficiencies Module 3 — “It takes a village”: Building active and professional relationships with parents and carers for effective literacy and numeracy learning. These online modules will: engage you in key subject matter and explicit teaching strategies, specific to their focus require you to apply your learning in cross-curricular as well as teaching area contexts provide opportunities to check your understanding as you progress through each module require you to complete a timed assessment at the end of each module. To complete each timed assessment, you will need to apply your knowledge and understanding of the specific module focus, by responding to multiple choice and/or short response items on related stimulus materials (e.g. a scenario or problem, an excerpt from a student response, a teaching artefact, other). Your participation in lectures and tutorials, and engagement with course readings and other Blackboard resources, should also be used to consolidate your knowledge and understanding of core content. More information on how to approach and complete these modules will be made available via the course Blackboard. Focusing Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (APSTs) for this assessment — Graduate career stage : 2.1, 2.5, 3.7 and 7.3. Core Content Learning Outcomes assessed: 2.4: Appropriate subject matter expertise in the effective teaching of literacy, including the explicit teaching of phonics in early reading and the explicit teaching of reading and writing in subjects other than English. 2.5: Appropriate subject matter expertise in the effective teaching of numeracy including the explicit teaching of mathematics and building of fluency, understanding, problem solving and reasoning. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
If you do not pass a timed assessment, you may reattempt until the due date and time.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please note, if requesting a discretionary extension on this assessment, it can apply to only one module and its timed assessment task.
Late submission
Each module will close on the due date. You will not be able to complete or reattempt after this time, unless you have an approved extension via myUQ.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: Each module will close on the due date. You will not be able to complete or reattempt after this time, unless you have an approved extension via myUQ.

assessment_details.title: AT2 Research essay — literacy teaching and learning
assessment_details.task_description: Write an essay in which you analyse two practical teaching strategies for explicit literacy instruction in one of your teaching areas, and address how these strategies could be used in a culturally responsive classroom to support students with English as an additional language/dialect (EAL/D). Your essay will need to: demonstrate knowledge and understanding of the literacy demands of your teaching area, drawing on relevant curriculum documents (e.g. from the ACARA, the QCAA) focus on one practical teaching strategy for explicit reading instruction and one for explicit writing instruction , ensuring each: --- specifically targets the literacy demands of your nominated teaching area --- is informed by current theories associated with literacy teaching and learning --- is aligned with course content (e.g. explicit teaching and learning for vocabulary acquisition, reading comprehension, fluency, genre-based approach, other) discuss and justify your selected strategies using current theories as well as relevant curriculum documents discuss how these strategies could be used in a culturally responsive classroom, to support literacy learning of students with EAL/D. In preparing and submitting your response, ensure you: use a minimum of five course readings use other recent peer-reviewed literature (published within the past five years, unless the document is a seminal work in the field of study) write using academic prose, without images or bullet-points. Given the focus and purpose of this essay, you may use sub-headings and/or first person use APA7 referencing conventions to acknowledge all sources, including a generative AI/machine translation technology statement. See the UQ Library guide and course Blackboard for more information. Focusing Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (APSTs) for this assessment — Graduate career stage : 2.1 and 2.5. Core Content Learning Outcome/s and Focus Area/s assessed: 2.4: Appropriate subject matter expertise in the effective teaching of literacy, including the explicit teaching of phonics in early reading and the explicit teaching of reading and writing in subjects other than English. Focus Area: Cultural responsiveness, including EAL/D students. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission to TurnItIn via the course Blackboard by the due date and time.
Reference list must include a completed generative AI/machine translation technology statement from the course Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If needed, you can apply to UQ's usual conditions for assessment extensions , including a request and use of one discretionary extension for this course.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: AT3 Design and presentation of a numeracy lesson plan
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation
assessment_details.weight: 50% — Completed template + 5-7 min presentation; up to 2000 words + reference list/appendices
assessment_details.due_date: Part 1: Lesson design, spoken rationale and peer feedback scheduled 5/05/2026 - 14/05/2026 Part 2: Final lesson design and account due 2pm 29/05/2026
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This task requires you to engage in an iterative process of lesson design for numeracy teaching and learning in one of your teaching areas. For Parts 1 and 2 of this task, you will need to use: the templates and submission advice provided in Blackboard to prepare and submit your response use APA7 referencing conventions to acknowledge all sources, including a generative AI/machine translation technology statement. See the UQ Library guide and course Blackboard for more information. Engagement with the course reading list, Blackboard resources, and activities in lectures and tutorials, should be used to consolidate your understanding of task requirements. Part 1: Lesson design and spoken rationale Design a 50-minute lesson for a middle school class that targets the numeracy demands of one of your teaching areas. Then present your design to an audience of fellow pre-service teachers for peer feedback. You will need to design your lesson using: the Australian Curriculum v9.0 (Years 7 to 10) the 21st Century Numeracy Model (see Goos, et. al., 2019)* your understanding of effective numeracy teaching, learning and task design from course readings and activities, including explicit teaching of mathematics and building of fluency, understanding, problem solving and reasoning a range of teaching strategies and resources, including ICTs, to support learning and engage students. To present your design, you will need to: prepare a spoken rationale of 5-7 minutes to deliver in your scheduled tutorial --- detailing your lesson and how it targets the numeracy demands of your chosen teaching area --- justifying your design choices, supported by curriculum documents and course reading on numeracy --- including visuals where appropriate, e.g., in sharing and making clear your lesson design be prepared to respond to questions from your peers and tutor at the end of your presentation. You are also required to provide feedback on your peers’ lesson designs in your scheduled tutorials. Presentations will be recorded for moderation purposes. Part 2: Final lesson design and account Using peer feedback and design requirements, critically evaluate and finalise your numeracy lesson. Then write an account of your final design, explicit enough for understanding and implementation by a teacher in your teaching area. You will need to: return to and evaluate your lesson design from Part 1, drawing on peer feedback and your understanding of the review process and design requirements. refine your lesson design as needed, ensuring you document changes made, what informed these changes and how they have improved the design of your numeracy lesson. develop and submit a written analysis of your final numeracy lesson, including justification of refinements you made in its design. Focusing Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (APSTs) for this assessment — Graduate career stage : 2.1, 2.5 and 3.4. Core Content Learning Outcome/s assessed: 2.5: Appropriate subject matter expertise in the effective teaching of numeracy including the explicit teaching of mathematics and building of fluency, understanding, problem solving and reasoning. *Goos, M., Geiger, V., Dole, S., Forgasz, H., & Bennison, A. (2019). Numeracy across the curriculum: research-based strategies for enhancing teaching and learning . Allen & Unwin. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Part 1: Completed template uploaded to TurnItIn. In-class presentation and feedback.
Part 2: Upload to TurnItIn.
Referencing for Part 1 and Part 2 must include completed generative AI/machine translation technology statements from the course Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If needed, you can apply for extensions via myUQ using the information below, in conjunction with UQ's conditions for assessment extensions .
Part 1 is an authentication task for this course. Any applications for extension must take into account task requirements for live delivery and peer feedback in scheduled tutorials, for you and the cohort.
The Course Coordinator will be consulted to confirm new dates and times for individuals with approved extensions to deliver their spoken rationales. Available dates and times will be subject to the cohort schedule for delivery, to be determined early in Semester 1. If there is no availability in an individual's timetabled tutorial, they may need to present for another tutorial group and tutor on a different day and/or time to their timetabled tutorial.
Please note, approved extensions on Part 1 will not cover the Part 2: Final design and account. Part 2 extensions also need to be requested via myUQ.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: AT4 — Part 1: Policies and procedures document for professional experience
assessment_details.task_description: This course requires you to complete a 10-day block (two week) supervised professional experience placement in a school. To undertake this placement, you need to: read the UQ School of Education’s Policies and Procedures for Professional Experiences . after you have read the policies and procedures document, complete and submit an acknowledgement via the course Blackboard, indicating that you have read and will abide by these policies and procedures while representing the University of Queensland on placement. Focusing Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (APSTs) for this assessment — Graduate career stage : 7.1 and 7.2. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The online acknowledgement will be available for completion and submission in the course Blackboard 'Assessment' section from Monday 16 February (O Week).
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Contact the Course Coordinator regarding potential extension for this task.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: AT4 — Part 2: Professional experience placement & final evaluation report
assessment_details.due_date: Professional experience placement (10 days) 8/06/2026 - 19/06/2026 Final evaluation report due 2pm 19/06/2026
assessment_details.task_description: Part 2 of the AT4 requires you to: successfully undertake a 10-day block (two weeks) of supervised professional experience, at a school assigned to you by the School of Education at the end of your placement, upload your final evaluation report to the course Blackboard. Before upload, this report must be completed by your School Coordinator and Teacher Supervisor/s. EDUC2630 observation and teaching load requirements are detailed on the UQ School of Education Professional Experience (PEx) site . To the AT4 Part 1, you need to have read and to abide by the UQ School of Education's Policies and Procedures for Professional Experience Placements , including attendance and completion requirements. Before and on placement, you should continue to refer to this document. Further information and resources are provided on the UQ School of Education PEx site for Students . Targeted resources and activities to support your preparation will also be provided in the 'Professional Experience' section of Blackboard and course teaching, learning and assessment. Focusing Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (APSTs) for this assessment — Graduate career stage : 1.1, 2.5, 4.2, 7.1 and 7.2. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
It is your responsibility to collect and upload your final evaluation report to the course Blackboard by the due date and time. It must include your own dated signature, as well as the dated signatures of your School Coordinator and Teacher Supervisor/s.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If completing make up days within the requirements of the UQ policies and procedures document, and this affects the final date of your placement, you will need to notify your Course Coordinator and upload your final evaluation report by 2pm on that date.
Refer to the School of Education's Policies and Procedures for Professional Experience Placements for more information on illness preventing attendance and circumstances for which make up days and alternative completion dates can and cannot be sought.
Late submission
Submitting late without prior negotiation with the course coordinator will result in a failing grade for the assessment and course.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Online engagement and preparation Setting yourself up to succeed in EDUC2630 Welcome to the course Blackboard Course scope and sequence Introduction to the teaching team Teaching activities and assessment requirements Preparing for professional experience placement

An introduction to literacy and numeracy: general capabilities for the 21st century Defining literacy and numeracy: an intersection of theory, research, curriculum and policy History and evolution of literacy and numeracy education, including the digital age Reading, writing and numeracy across the age range, socioeconomic and cultural contexts The role of critical literacy and numeracy in personal enablement and social empowerment The value of discipline-specific expertise and cross-curricular communities of practice Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L07

An introduction to literacy & numeracy: challenges and possibilities for teaching and learning Personal perceptions and experiences of literacy and numeracy as learners and pre-service teachers Defining and identifying literacy and numeracy in and out of the classroom The value of discipline-specific expertise and cross-curricular communities of practice The role of critical literacy and numeracy in personal enablement and social empowerment of secondary students in the 21st century Due 2pm Friday, Week 1: AT4 Part 1 — Policies and procedures document for professional experience Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L07

Literacy and numeracy in and across the curriculum Literacy and numeracy in the Australian Curriculum v9.0 as general capabilities and their relationship with the English and Mathematics strands and proficiencies The Australian Curriculum v9.0 literacy and numeracy learning progressions Literacy and numeracy as complementary skills in the QCAA senior syllabuses Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L07

Cross-curricular and subject-specific demands and opportunities Identifying literacy and numeracy demands and opportunities in teaching areas: Using the Australian Curriculum v9.0 Year level content descriptions and elaborations, the literacy and numeracy general capabilities and their learning progressions Literacy and numeracy as complementary skills in the QCAA senior syllabuses Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 5 (09 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Cross-curricular and disciplinary literacy teaching and learning Lectures 3 to 5: Listening, speaking, reading, writing and designing across the curriculum, in contemporary (including digital) contexts Traditional, Socio constructivist, functional and genre-based approaches to literacy teaching and learning With the brain in mind — the relationship between reading and writing, and the importance of systematic and explicit teaching Phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, vocabulary acquisition, comprehension and oral language Writing structures and conventions, grammar, punctuation and spelling Meeting the demands of discipline-specific and increasingly complex texts in secondary schooling Literacy (and an introduction to numeracy) for learners with English as an additional language/dialect, including literacy development to access the curriculum and respecting and caring for Funds of Knowledge and Funds of Identity in Culturally Responsive literacy teaching Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 5 (09 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Strategies for literacy teaching and learning across the curriculum Tutorials 3 to 5: Creating healthy listening, speaking, reading, writing and designing classroom cultures Identifying and meeting the reading and writing demands and opportunities of the curriculum and subject disciplines Research-based activities and strategies for reading and writing across the teaching and learning cycle Using ICTs to enhance students' literacy learning and development The ACARA EAL/D learning progression and the importance of cognitive academic language proficiency (CALP) Valuing and mobilising cultural difference, repertoires and intelligences for literacy learning Due 2pm Friday, Week 4: AT1 Module 1 — Effective reading instruction using phonemic awareness and phonics Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 6 To Week 8 (30 Mar - 26 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Cross-curricular and disciplinary numeracy teaching and learning Lectures 6 to 8: Recognising and overcoming learner and teacher “maths anxiety” Understanding the Mathematics strands and proficiencies, and their relationship to numeracy in the Australian Curriculum v9.0 Effective instruction of mathematical concepts and skills within and beyond the mathematics curriculum Numeracy as context-based, sociocultural practice and the need for a cross-disciplinary 21st century approach Understanding and modelling “mathematical noticing” and “mathematical thinking” The significance of ICTs in 21st century numeracy and numeracy teaching and learning The relationship between numeracy and literacy across the curriculum Numeracy for learners with English as an additional language/dialect Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 6 To Week 8 (30 Mar - 26 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Strategies for numeracy teaching and learning across the curriculum Tutorials 6 to 8: Recognising and overcoming learner and teacher “maths anxiety”, for healthy classroom numeracy cultures Identifying and meeting the numeracy demands and opportunities of the curriculum and subject disciplines Applying the 21st Century Numeracy Model, other research-based strategies, "mathematical noticing" and "mathematical thinking" in classrooms Using the 21st Century Numeracy Model to design rich and authentic numeracy tasks in teaching areas, including the use of ICTs Explicit instruction and gradual release of scaffolding to develop students as independent problem-solvers Valuing and mobilising cultural difference, repertoires and intelligences for numeracy learning Please note, the mid-semester break (below) falls between Teaching Weeks 6 and 7. Due 2pm Friday, Week 7: AT2 Research essay Due 2pm Friday, Week 8: AT1 Module 2 — Explicit numeracy teaching and learning: Mathematics strands and proficiencies Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 10 (27 Apr - 10 May)
learning_activities.topic: Literacy and numeracy for diverse learners Lectures 9 and 10: The value of designing, shaping and critically evaluating literacy and numeracy teaching and learning as an iterative process Unpacking the data and unboxing the literacy and numeracy learner: gender, socioeconomic status, linguistic and cultural differences Deficit and strength-based approaches to literacy and numeracy teaching and learning, including differentiating literacy and numeracy teaching and learning and Universal Design for Learning Co-constructing First Nations perspectives and knowledges in literacy and numeracy teaching and learning Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 10 (27 Apr - 10 May)
learning_activities.topic: Teaching literacy and numeracy in diverse classrooms / AT3 Spoken presentations Tutorial 9 — Case study workshop: Deficit and strengths-based approaches to literacy and numeracy learners and learning in diverse classrooms Differentiating literacy and numeracy teaching and learning to meet the needs and strengths of students The potential of Universal Design for Learning in literacy and numeracy teaching and learning Tutorial 10: AT3 Part 1 — Spoken rationales and peer feedback Due 9am Tuesday, Week 10: AT3 Part 1 — lesson design, reference list and speaking notes Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 11 To Week 13 (11 May - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Assessing literacy and numeracy / "It takes a village" Lectures 11 to 13: Diagnostic, formative and summative assessment of literacy and numeracy The role and use of national and international testing, e.g., NAPLAN, PISA Meeting numeracy and literacy requirements for the Queensland Certificate of Education Strategies for active and professional relationships with parents and carers Whole-school approaches to literacy and numeracy teaching, learning and assessment Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 11 To Week 13 (11 May - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: AT3 Spoken presentations / Using literacy and numeracy data and assessment Tutorial 11: AT3 Part 1 — Spoken rationales and peer feedback Tutorials 12 and 13 — Case study workshops: Working with the Australian Curriculum v9.0 literacy and numeracy progressions, student responses, and student and school NAPLAN data student, teacher, school and parent/carer perceptions of literacy, numeracy, testing and reporting Using literacy and numeracy assessment and data in whole-school approaches to improving literacy and numeracy outcomes differentiating literacy and numeracy teaching and learning to meet the needs and strengths of students active and professional relationships with parents/carers Due 2pm Friday, Week 12: AT1 Module 3 — Active and professional relationships with parents and carers for effective literacy and numeracy learning Due 2pm Friday, Week 13: AT3 Final account and design Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Exam week 1 To Exam week 2 (08 Jun - 21 Jun)
learning_activities.topic: Professional Experience Placement (10 days) Due 2pm Friday, Exam Week 2 (final day of placement): AT4 Part 2 — Final evaluation report Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06, L07, L08

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Fitness to Practise Policy Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy Incidental Student Fees and Charges Procedure Student Travel Procedure Learning activities
